questions.text: The gene which controls the ABO group has how many alleles in an individual?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not correct.we have only 2 genes for blood group. because one person can have only 2 alleles at a time
This is correct we have only 2 genes for blood group. because one person can have only 2 alleles at a time
we have only 2 genes for blood group. because one person can have only 2 alleles at a time
This option is not correct. we have only 2 genes for blood group. because one person can have only 2 alleles at a time

questions.explanation: ABO blood groups in humans are controlled by the gene I. It has three alleles: IA, IB, and i. Since, there are three different alleles, six different genotypes are possible. However, a single individual has two ABO blood type alleles, because we each inherit one blood type allele from our biological mother and one from our biological father. Hence, the correct answer is B.

questions.text: “Law of independent assortment” states:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: That each pair of alleles assort independently of other pairs of alleles during gamete formation
options.explanation: This statement accurately represents the Law of Independent Assortment. It means that genes located on different chromosomes are inherited independently of each other during gamete formation.

options.text: That alleles of each pair of contrasting trait have unequal probability to assort with the alleles of other pair
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. The Law of Independent Assortment states that alleles segregate independently, regardless of the alleles at other gene loci. The probability of assorting is equal for all alleles.

options.text: That the two coexisting alleles for each trait segregate (separate) from each other at meiosis so that each gamete receives only one of the two alleles
options.explanation: This statement describes the Law of Segregation, not the Law of Independent Assortment. The Law of Segregation states that each individual has two alleles for each gene, and these alleles segregate during the formation of gametes.

options.text: That pertain to inheritance of single trait (monohybrid cross)
options.explanation: The Law of Independent Assortment is not specific to a single trait but applies to genes located on different chromosomes. The term "monohybrid cross" is more closely associated with the Law of Segregation.

questions.explanation: Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another. In other words, the allele a gamete receives for one gene does not influence the allele received for another gene.

questions.text: In 'Complete Dominance':
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Different alleles of a gene are both expressed in heterozygous condition
options.explanation: This describes codominance, not complete dominance.In codominance, both alleles in a heterozygote are fully expressed — for example, in human AB blood group, both A and B alleles are expressed equally.However, in complete dominance, only one allele (the dominant one) shows its effect, while the recessive allele is completely masked.

options.text: One allele (R) is completely dominant over the other (r)
options.explanation: This is the definition of complete dominance.In this case, the dominant allele (R) completely masks the expression of the recessive allele (r) in a heterozygous individual (Rr).Example:In pea plants, the allele for round seeds (R) is dominant over wrinkled seeds (r).So both RR and Rr genotypes result in round seeds.

options.text: The phenotype of the heterozygote is intermediate between phenotypes of the two homozygotes
options.explanation: This describes incomplete dominance, not complete dominance.In incomplete dominance, neither allele is completely dominant; the heterozygous phenotype is a blend of both alleles.Example:In snapdragon flowers, a red (RR) and white (rr) parent produce pink (Rr) offspring.

options.text: Gene mutations may produce many different alleles of a gene
options.explanation: While gene mutations can indeed create multiple alleles, this has no relation to the concept of complete dominance.Complete dominance only describes how two existing alleles interact, not how alleles are formed through mutation.

questions.explanation: Complete dominance is a form of dominance in a heterozygous condition wherein the allele that is regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. Option C refers to 'Incomplete Dominance' where a dominant allele, or form of a gene, does not completely mask the effects of a recessive allele, and the organism's resulting physical appearance shows a blending of both alleles.

questions.text: The genotype of blood group 'AB' in humans is _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the human blood group system, there are four main types: A, B, AB, and O. Each individual inherits two alleles for the ABO blood group, one from each parent. The A and B alleles are codominant, meaning that both are expressed when they are both present in an individual's genotype. The O allele is recessive, so it is only expressed when an individual has two copies of the O allele. The genotype of blood group AB is IAIB, which means that the individual has inherited one A allele and one B allele from their parents. These two alleles are codominant, so both the A and B antigens are expressed on the surface of their red blood cells. Individuals with blood group AB are considered universal recipients because they can receive blood from individuals with any blood group without causing a transfusion reaction. However, they can only donate blood to individuals with blood group AB.
This genotype represents blood group B, as it has the B allele from one parent and an O allele from the other parent.
This genotype represents blood group A, as it has the A allele from both parents.
This genotype represents blood group O, as it has an O allele from both parents.

questions.explanation: ABO blood type in humans is determined by 3 alleles IA, IB (Dominant), and i (Recessive) Genotype IA shows blood group A. Genotype TB shows blood group B. Genotype Io shows blood group O. But, IA & IB are co-dominant alleles means neither of the dominant alleles can mask the expression of other dominant alleles. So, the genotype IA IB shows blood group AB.

questions.text: Which one is not a continuous trait?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Human height has a range of valuesHuman height is often treated as a continuous trait, meaning that it exists on a spectrum and can vary across a range of values. In a given population, you can typically observe a distribution of heights where individuals fall along a continuum from shorter to taller. The continuous nature of height is a result of the polygenic inheritance pattern, which means that multiple genes contribute to determining a person's height. These genes interact with each other and with environmental factors, such as nutrition and health, to influence the final outcome. While height is predominantly continuous, it is often convenient to categorize individuals into discrete groups for certain purposes, such as clinical assessments or statistical analysis. These categories, such as "tall," "average," and "short," are arbitrary thresholds imposed on a continuous trait for convenience or specific applications. However, it is important to note that these categories are not universally defined and can vary across cultures and contexts.

Discontinuous trait; you can either roll your tongue or you can’tTongue rolling, also known as the ability to roll or curl the tongue into a tube shape, is typically considered a genetic trait that exhibits a simple Mendelian inheritance pattern. It is often described as a binary trait, meaning that individuals are classified as either "tongue rollers" or "non-tongue rollers." The ability to roll the tongue is determined by a single gene locus called the "T" locus. Individuals who inherit at least one dominant allele at this locus are typically able to roll their tongues, while those who inherit two recessive alleles are unable to roll their tongues. This pattern suggests a single genetic factor with a dominant-recessive relationship. Although tongue rolling is commonly taught in biology classes as a simple Mendelian trait, there is some debate about the exact genetics involved and whether it is influenced by additional genes or environmental factors. Some studies suggest that the inheritance pattern may be more complex, with multiple genes contributing to the trait. However, overall, tongue rolling is often treated as a discrete trait rather than a continuous one.

Wheat grain colour has a range of coloursWheat grain color is typically considered a discrete trait rather than a continuous trait. Wheat grain color can vary among different varieties and can be classified into distinct categories such as red, white, or yellow, among others. The color of wheat grain is determined by the presence or absence of specific pigments, such as anthocyanins or carotenoids, which are influenced by genetic factors. Different alleles at specific gene loci can result in variations in grain color. For example, in some cases, the presence of pigments leads to red or dark-colored grains, while the absence of pigments results in white or yellow grains. While there can be some variation within each color category, the overall categorization of wheat grain color into discrete groups is commonly used in agricultural research, breeding programs, and the industry. This discrete classification allows for easier identification, selection, and breeding of wheat varieties with specific grain colors to meet various market demands or agronomic requirements.

Human skin colour has a range of coloursHuman skin color is a complex trait influenced by multiple genetic and environmental factors. It exhibits a wide range of variation across populations, and this variation is often described as a continuum rather than discrete categories. The color of human skin is primarily determined by the amount and distribution of a pigment called melanin, which is produced by specialized cells called melanocytes. Melanin comes in two main forms: eumelanin, which is brown-black, and pheomelanin, which is yellow-red. The relative proportions and distribution of these two types of melanin, along with other factors such as blood flow and collagen content, contribute to the observed variation in skin color. Skin color is influenced by multiple genes, and variations in these genes can lead to differences in the production, distribution, and activity of melanin. However, the inheritance of skin color is not as straightforward as a single gene with clear dominant or recessive alleles. Instead, it involves the interaction of multiple genes and complex inheritance patterns. Due to the continuous nature of human skin color variation, it is often more accurate to describe it as a spectrum or gradient rather than discrete categories. The concept of discrete racial or ethnic categories based on skin color is a social construct that does not fully capture the complexity and diversity of human populations.

questions.explanation: A continuous trait displays a range of expression (such as weight, height, etc.) rather than an all-or-none appearance (such as white or red)

questions.text: Linked genes with closer loci _ Mendel’s law of independent assortment as they _ assort independently.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Linked genes with closer loci do not obey the law as they get assorted on the same side due to less distance between them
Mendel’s law states that independent assortment takes place hence if it could assort independently, it would obey the law
Mendel’s law states that independent assortment takes place hence if it could assort independently, it would obey the law
They do not obey the law

questions.explanation: Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another

questions.text: Ahmed’s blood CANNOT clot properly; he was diagnosed with hemophilia A. He passed this trait to his grandson through his:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The wife is not Ahmed’s offspring, and the question asks how Ahmed passed the trait to his grandson.Although the wife provides X chromosomes to her own children, Ahmed’s hemophilia gene must be transmitted through his own X chromosome, not via his wife.So, this option is irrelevant.

A son inherits the Y chromosome from his father and the X chromosome from his mother.Since hemophilia A is X-linked recessive, Ahmed’s X chromosome (with the defective gene) cannot go to his son — it only goes to his daughter.Hence, Ahmed cannot pass hemophilia directly to his son.

Hemophilia A is X-linked recessive, meaning the gene for this disorder is located on the X chromosome.Ahmed (hemophiliac male) has the genotype XʰY.He passes his Xʰ chromosome (with hemophilia) to his daughter, because daughters always inherit their father’s X and their mother’s X.The daughter becomes a carrier (XʰX), usually healthy but able to pass the gene on.When this carrier daughter has a son, he inherits her affected Xʰ and his father’s Y — making him XʰY, a hemophiliac male.Therefore, Ahmed’s grandson inherits hemophilia through Ahmed’s daughter.
Ahmed’s brother is not his descendant, and therefore Ahmed cannot pass the gene through him.This option is unrelated to the inheritance line leading to Ahmed’s grandson.

questions.explanation: X chromosome of the father is only transferred to the daughter, son takes the Y chromosome and haemophilia is a X-linked diseaseAhmed, who has hemophilia A, a condition where blood does not clot properly, can pass this trait to his grandson through his daughter. Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive disorder, meaning that the gene responsible for the condition is located on the X chromosome.Since Ahmed is the grandfather, he must pass on his X chromosome to his daughter. If his daughter inherits the affected X chromosome from Ahmed, she becomes a carrier of hemophilia A. As a carrier, she has one normal X chromosome and one affected X chromosome, but since hemophilia A is a recessive trait, she does not show symptoms of the condition.When the daughter has a child (Ahmed's grandson), she has a 50% chance of passing on the affected X chromosome to her son. If the affected X chromosome is passed on, the grandson would inherit the hemophilia A trait and potentially experience clotting problems.It's important to note that if the daughter does not pass on the affected X chromosome to her son, he will not inherit hemophilia A. However, he would still be a carrier of the condition, meaning he could potentially pass it on to his own offspring if he has children in the future.

questions.text: The total number of genes present in a particular population, at a particular time is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A gene is a specific segment of DNA that contains the instructions for building a particular protein or determining a specific trait. However, the total number of genes present in a population is not referred to as a "gene."

The genome refers to the complete set of genetic material (DNA) present in an organism. It includes all the genes, non-coding sequences, and regulatory elements. The genome encompasses the entire genetic information of an individual or a species.

Genotype refers to the genetic constitution of an individual, specifically the combination of alleles present in an organism's DNA. It represents the genetic information that determines the observable traits (phenotype) of an organism. However, the term "genotype" does not describe the total number of genes in a population.

Gene pool refers to the sum total of all the genes, alleles, and their variations present in a population at a particular time. It includes all the genetic information and diversity available within a population. The gene pool of a population is subject to change due to various factors such as mutation, migration, genetic drift, and natural selection.

A karyotype is a visual representation of the chromosomes of an individual or a species, arranged in a specific order. It allows for examining the number, structure, and size of chromosomes. While a karyotype provides valuable information about the organization of chromosomes, it does not describe the total number of genes in a population.

questions.explanation: Gene pool refers to the sum total of all the genes, alleles, and their variations present in a population at a particular time. It includes all the genetic information and diversity available within a population. The gene pool of a population is subject to change due to various factors such as mutation, migration, genetic drift, and natural selection.

questions.text: The type of gene interaction in which the effect caused by a gene at one locus interferes with the effect caused by another gene at another locus is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pleiotropy is a gene interaction in which a single gene affects multiple traits.
The type of gene interaction in which the effect caused by a gene at one locus interferes with the effect caused by another gene at another locus is known as epistasis.
Polygenic inheritance is a gene interaction in which multiple genes contribute to a single trait.
Gene linkage is a phenomenon in which genes that are located close together on a chromosome are inherited together more often than would be expected by chance.
Crossing over is a process that occurs during meiosis in which homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material.

questions.explanation: Option A: Pleiotropy is a gene interaction in which a single gene affects multiple traits. Option B: The type of gene interaction in which the effect caused by a gene at one locus interferes with the effect caused by another gene at another locus is known as epistasis. Option C: Polygenic inheritance is a gene interaction in which multiple genes contribute to a single trait. Option D: Gene linkage is a phenomenon in which genes that are located close together on a chromosome are inherited together more often than would be expected by chance. Option E: Crossing over is a process that occurs during meiosis in which homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material.

questions.text: Two parents, one hemophilic and the other carrier female, chances among the male offspring to be hemophilic:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that only one-fourth (25%) of the male offspring would be hemophilic.However, when we perform the cross between XʰY × XᴴXʰ,

From this cross:50% males are normal (XᴴY)50% males are hemophilic (XʰY)Therefore, half (50%) of the male offspring will inherit the affected X chromosome from the carrier mother and become hemophilic.

This would mean that three out of four male offspring are hemophilic.This is incorrect, because there are only two possibilities for sons—either normal (XᴴY) or hemophilic (XʰY)—and both occur in equal proportion (50-50).
This would mean all sons are hemophilic, which is not true in this cross.For 100% of the sons to be hemophilic, every X chromosome that could be passed on to the sons must carry the defective gene.

questions.explanation: In the extremely rare event that both the mother and father have the affected X chromosomes, there is a 50% chance that their sons will be born with hemophilia. There will be a 50% chance that their daughters will be carriers and a 50% chance of them being hemophilic.50%The hemophilic parent is presumably the father, since hemophilia is an X-linked recessive trait. The carrier female parent has one normal X chromosome and one X chromosome with the hemophilia gene.The male offspring have a 50% chance of inheriting the X chromosome with the hemophilia gene, making them hemophilic. The other 50% of the time, they will inherit the normal X chromosome and be unaffected.The female offspring have a 50% chance of being carriers like their mother, and a 50% chance of being unaffected.

questions.text: Which of the following blood transfusions can be made safely?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If blood group A is transfused into group B, the donor i.e. A antigens(RBCs) will be agglutinated by the recipient's anti-A antibodies
Group AB is considered a universal acceptor as it does not contain any antibodies to A or B in their blood so transfusions can be made safely from any blood group type.
Blood group O can only receive transfusions from O because it has antibodies to A and B. Any other transfusion can cause agglutination reactions.
O can receive blood only from O because it contains antibodies to A and B which cause serious complications.

questions.explanation: Blood Group B can easily be transfused to the person having blood group AB, the reason being the person having blood group AB will have antigens of A as well as B on its cells, hence the body recognizes both A and B.

questions.text: Blood group AB is an example of _ .
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Complete dominance is a form of dominance in the heterozygous condition wherein the allele that is regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. For instance, for an individual carrying two alleles that are both dominant (e.g. AA), the trait that they represent will be expressed.
A recessive allele does not produce a trait at all when only one copy is present. This contrasts to a dominant trait, which requires that only one of the two alleles be present to express the trait.
Codominance, as it relates to genetics, refers to a type of inheritance in which two versions (alleles) of the same gene are expressed separately to yield different traits in an individual.

Incomplete dominance is a form of Gene interaction in which both alleles of a gene at a locus are partially expressed, often resulting in an intermediate or different phenotype. It is also known as partial dominance.

questions.explanation: Option A describes one allele being completely dominant over the other allele i.e. one allele is dominant while the other is recessive so, in a heterozygous condition, only the dominant allele is expressed as a trait. However, in the AB blood group, BOTH A and B antigens are expressed so, that one allele is not completely dominant over the other. Option B describes alleles that are only expressed in the phenotype in homozygous conditions; they will not be expressed if a dominant allele is present in heterozygous conditions. One allele is recessive over the other, but in this case, both traits are expressed. Option C is the correct answer, as it describes the scenario in which both alleles are equally expressed in the phenotype. Since both antigens are expressed individually, this means that both alleles are equally expressed. Option D describes a scenario in which the alleles are expressed in such a way that the phenotype is actually a mixture of intermediate expression of both alleles. The individual alleles are not expressed completely. For example, alleles of black and white fur color are not expressed as black and white spots, they are expressed as grey fur color, i.e. an intermediate or mixture of the two traits.

questions.text: Rh⁻ individuals have genotypes:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This genotype contains one dominant (D) and one recessive (d) allele.Since the dominant allele D expresses the Rh antigen, individuals with Dd genotype will be Rh⁺, not Rh⁻.In genetics, the presence of just one dominant allele is enough to show the dominant trait.Thus, Dd = Rh⁺ individual, not Rh⁻.
This option would only be correct if none of the other genotypes (A, C, D) matched the Rh⁻ condition.However, as we will see, dd perfectly explains Rh⁻ individuals, so “None of these” is not correct.

Here, both alleles are dominant (D).This means the person has a strong presence of the Rh antigen on their red blood cells.Therefore, individuals with DD genotype are Rh⁺, not Rh⁻.So, this genotype represents Rh-positive blood, not Rh-negative.

This is the homozygous recessive condition — both alleles are d (no dominant D present).As a result, no Rh antigen appears on the red blood cells, making the person Rh-negative (Rh⁻).Hence, individuals with dd genotype do not express the Rh factor and are Rh⁻.

questions.explanation: Rh blood group is determined by the D/d gene, in which D is completely dominant over d. Rh- is a homozygous recessive trait and requires the genotype to be dd.

questions.text: Which of the following conditions is hybrid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is incorrect as Hybrid means “Heterozygous gene” and this is homozygous.
B is correct as Hybrid means “Heterozygous gene” i.e Gene that has two different alleles. E.g Aa or Yy.
This is incorrect as Hybrid means “Heterozygous gene” and this is homozygous.
Only option B is correct.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct as "Aa" shows that the gene has two different alleles so it is heterozygous or hybrid.

questions.text: Red-green color-blindness is an X-linked recessive disorder. Jacob's paternal grandfather and father are both color-blind, but his mother has two normal alleles. What is the probability that Jacob is red-green color-blind?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Since Jacob's mother has two normal alleles, he cannot inherit the color-blind gene from her. His father only passes a Y chromosome to him, not an X chromosome. Therefore, the probability is 0%.
This percentage is incorrect because it assumes a different probability of inheriting the X-linked trait. However, Jacob's mother cannot pass a color-blind allele to him.
This percentage is incorrect. It suggests that there is a 50% chance Jacob could inherit a color-blind allele from his mother, but she has two normal alleles and cannot pass a color-blind gene.
This percentage is incorrect as it suggests an even higher probability of inheriting a color-blind allele, but Jacob's mother has no color-blind alleles to pass on to him.

questions.explanation: In humans, red-green color-blindness is an X-linked recessive trait. Males (XY) will be color-blind if the gene is present on only their single X chromosome. Therefore a color-blind boy can’t receive a color-blind gene from his father, even if his father is color-blind because his father can only pass an X chromosome to his daughters.

questions.text: When both the alleles of a gene pair are same, the organism is said to be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heterozygous – This refers to an organism having two different alleles for a gene (Aa), not the same.
Genotype – This refers to the genetic makeup of an organism, whether homozygous or heterozygous, but does not specifically mean that both alleles are the same.
An organism is homozygous when both alleles of a gene pair are the same, either dominant (AA) or recessive (aa).Example: A plant with TT (tall) or tt (short) alleles for height is homozygous.
Phenotype – This is the observable trait of an organism (e.g., tall or short), not the genetic composition.

questions.explanation: An allele is one of two, or more, forms of a given gene variant. Each person has at least two alleles for a specific gene. If they are the same then the organism is called Homozygous for that gene. (Option B is correct) And if they are different, they are called heterozygous for that allele.A genotype is an organism's set of heritable genes that can be passed down from parents to offspring. A phenotype is an individual's observable traits, such as height, eye color, and blood type.

questions.text: Read the passage below to answer the question: Haemophilia is a disorder in which blood fails to clot. Saad, a male haemophiliac, marries Sara, a normal woman and together they have four children, two boys (Ahmed and Ali) and two girls (Alia and Ayesha). None of the children displays the symptoms of Haemophilia. Ahmed, Ali, Ayesha, and Ali all marry normal individuals and have children. None of Ahmed's or Ali’s children, male or female, display symptoms of haemophilia, but the sons of Alia and Ayesha display symptoms of haemophilia while the daughters of Alia and Ayesha do not. Which of the following best explains the reason that Ahmed, Ali, Ayesha and Alia do not display symptoms of haemophilia, even though their father, Saad, is a haemophiliac?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Haemophilia is an X-linked disorder, and Saad can only pass on his Y chromosome
options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because Saad can pass his Y chromosomes to his sons and also pass his X chromosome to his daughters.

options.text: Haemophilia is an X-linked disorder, and even though Alia and Ayesha received a hemophiliac X-chromosome from Saad, Sara gave them a normal X-chromosome
options.explanation: Haemophilia is an X-linked recessive disorder. Now, based on the information that we have, Saad is haemophiliac so he must have an X chromosome with the haemophilia gene. Sara is normal, so she could either be homozygous dominant or heterozygous for the haemophilia gene. Since none of the four children display symptoms of haemophilia, we can conclude that Sara is indeed homozygous dominant as being heterozygous, half of the children have had the possibility of being haemophiliac. So, Ali and Ahmed each receive a Y chromosome from Saad and a normal copy of the X chromosome each from Sara. Alia and Ayesha receive a normal copy of the X chromosome from Sara and a haemophilia copy of the X chromosome from Saad, making them carriers of the disease. They do not have the disease themselves. Option B is correct as this is indeed the pattern of inheritance, as described above.

options.text: Haemophilia is a Y-linked disorder, and therefore cannot be displayed in females
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect as Haemophilia is an X-linked recessive disease and can be displayed in both males and females.

options.text: Haemophilia is a Y-linked disorder ,and Ahmed and Ali must have received an X Chromosome from Saad
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect because Haemophilia is an X-linked recessive disease and Ahmed and Ali received a Y chromosome from Saad, as they are males.

options.text: Haemophilia is an X-linked disorder, and even though Ahmed and All received a Hemophilia X chromosome from Saad, Sara gave them a normal X chromosome
options.explanation: Option E is incorrect because Ahmed and Ali received a Y chromosome from Saad.

questions.explanation: Haemophilia is an X-linked chromosome, not a Y-linked chromosome. Daughters of Sara and Saad, Alia and Ayesha receive an X chromosome from both of their parents. Even though hemophilia X-chromosome is received by these daughters from Saad they received normal X from their mother, because of which they do not show any symptoms. Option A is wrong because Saad can pass X as well as Y chromosomes to his offspring. Options C and D are wrong because it is not a Y-linked but an X-linked disease. Option E is wrong because Ahmed and Ali can not receive an X chromosome from Saad (their father), they should receive a Y chromosome, for them to be male.

questions.text: Mendel concluded that each organism has two hereditary factors for each trait, now called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chromatids are the identical halves of a duplicated chromosome. They are involved in cell division and do not directly relate to Mendel's hereditary factors.
Alleles are different forms of a gene that exist at the same position on homologous chromosomes. Mendel's hereditary factors, which we now know as genes, have different versions called alleles.
Chromosomes carry genetic information in the form of genes. While they are crucial for inheritance, they are not the specific hereditary factors Mendel described.
One of the provided options correctly identifies Mendel's hereditary factors, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Mendel's experiments led to the discovery that traits are inherited through discrete units, now known as alleles. Each organism inherits two alleles for each trait, one from each parent. While chromatids and chromosomes are part of the structure and function of genetic material, they do not refer to the specific hereditary units Mendel identified. Therefore, the correct answer is alleles.

questions.text: When phenotype of a heterozygote is in between the phenotypes of both the homozygous parents, it is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incomplete dominance occurs when the phenotype of the heterozygous individual is intermediate (a blend) between the phenotypes of the two homozygous parents.In this case, neither allele is completely dominant over the other.For example, in snapdragon flowers, when a red-flowered plant (RR) is crossed with a white-flowered plant (rr), the F₁ generation has pink flowers (Rr).This shows that the heterozygote’s phenotype is a mixture of the two parental phenotypes — not purely one or the other.Thus, incomplete dominance reflects partial expression of both alleles, producing an intermediate trait.

Epistasis is not about blending traits, but about interaction between genes at different loci.In epistasis, one gene masks or suppresses the effect of another gene, even if both are dominant.For example, in Labrador retrievers, a gene for pigment deposition (E) can hide the effect of the color gene (B); if a dog is homozygous recessive (ee), it will be yellow regardless of its B/b genotype.Therefore, epistasis affects how genes interact, not how alleles within a single gene blend — so this does not describe the intermediate phenotype seen in incomplete dominance.

Pleiotropy is when a single gene influences multiple, seemingly unrelated traits.For instance, in sickle-cell anemia, the defective hemoglobin gene affects red blood cell shape, oxygen transport, and resistance to malaria — all different aspects controlled by a single gene.This is completely different from incomplete dominance, which deals with the expression of two alleles for one trait.So pleiotropy refers to one gene → many effects, not two alleles → blended phenotype.

Codominance occurs when both alleles in a heterozygote are fully expressed — not blended.In this case, both parental traits appear side by side in the phenotype rather than mixing.A common example is human blood group AB, where both A and B alleles are expressed equally on red blood cells.Thus, in codominance, both traits are visible simultaneously, whereas in incomplete dominance, the traits mix to form an intermediate appearance.

questions.explanation: This type of relationship between alleles, with a heterozygote phenotype intermediate between the two homozygous phenotypes, is called incomplete dominance. In the question, "in between" is closest in meaning to "intermediate", thus incomplete dominance is the correct choice. Thus, the correct answer is 'Diphtheria, Leprosy, Plague.'

questions.text: _ are more common in human males than females.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: X-linked dominant trait has equal chances of appearing in both males and females.
X-linked recessive traits are more common in males because there is no other X chromosome to mask the effect of the trait, meaning even a single allele will cause the trait to appear.
Y-linked traits only appear in males because human females do not have the Y chromosome.
Autosomal traits (recessive or dominant) are not influenced by sex.

questions.explanation: Because it is unlikely that females will have two altered copies of this gene, males are affected by X-linked recessive disorders much more frequently than females.

questions.text: A trait whose alleles are present in both males and females but prominent more in one sex than other is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sex linked traits are those whose alleles are present on sex chromosomes (X and Y in humans).
The traits that are exclusively present in one sex are called sex-limited traits.
The traits that are more common in a particular sex are called sex-influenced traits.
X linked traits are those whose alleles are present on X chromosome only.

questions.text: Erythroblastosis foetalis occurs when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Mother is Rh-positive and Baby is Rh-negative
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Erythroblastosis fetalis occurs when an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive baby, not when both have different Rh factors with the mother being Rh-positive.

options.text: Mother is Rh-negative and the Baby is Rh-positive
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Erythroblastosis fetalis arises from Rh incompatibility when an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus, leading to potential hemolysis of fetal red blood cells if sensitization occurs.

options.text: Both Mother and Baby are Rh-negative
options.explanation: This is incorrect. If both mother and baby are Rh-negative, there is no Rh incompatibility, and thus erythroblastosis fetalis does not occur.

options.text: Both Mother and Baby are Rh-positive
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Rh incompatibility arises when the mother is Rh-negative and the baby is Rh-positive, not when both are Rh-positive.

options.text: All of the above statements are true
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Only the statement about an Rh-negative mother and an Rh-positive baby (Option B) is true in the context of erythroblastosis fetalis.

questions.explanation: Erythroblastosis fetalis occurs when an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus. The mother's immune system may recognize the Rh-positive red blood cells of the fetus as foreign and produce antibodies against them, leading to hemolysis. This condition typically arises during a second pregnancy, after the mother has been sensitized by a previous Rh-positive pregnancy. Options A, C, and D are incorrect because they describe scenarios where there is no Rh incompatibility. Option E is also incorrect as it incorrectly suggests that all scenarios could result in erythroblastosis fetalis, which is not the case.

questions.text: Hemophilia occurs because of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: “Dominant” means that a single copy of the mutated gene (from one parent) is enough to cause the disorder. A child of a person affected by an autosomal dominant condition has a 50% chance of being affected by that condition via inheritance of a dominant allele.

The human Y chromosome is particularly exposed to high mutation rates due to the environment in which it is housed. The Y chromosome is passed exclusively through sperm, which undergoes multiple cell divisions during gametogenesis. Each cellular division provides further opportunity to accumulate base pair mutations.

Hemophilia is a bleeding disorder that slows the blood clotting process. Hemophilia A and hemophilia B are inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern. The genes associated with these conditions are located on the X chromosome, which is one of the two sex chromosomes. In males (who have only one X chromosome), one altered copy of the gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the condition. In females (who have two X chromosomes), a mutation would have to occur in both copies of the gene to cause the disorder. Because it is unlikely that females will have two altered copies of this gene, it is very rare for females to have hemophilia. A characteristic of X-linked inheritance is that fathers cannot pass X-linked traits to their sons. So, the correct answer is 'Mutation of X-chromosome'.

Symptoms of iron-deficiency anemia are related to decreased oxygen delivery to the entire body and may include: Being pale or having yellow "sallow" skin. Unexplained fatigue or lack of energy. Shortness of breath or chest pain, especially with activity.

questions.explanation: Hemophilia is a bleeding disorder that slows the blood clotting process. Hemophilia A and hemophilia B are inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern. The genes associated with these conditions are located on the X chromosome, which is one of the two sex chromosomes. In males (who have only one X chromosome), one altered copy of the gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the condition. In females (who have two X chromosomes), a mutation would have to occur in both copies of the gene to cause the disorder. Because it is unlikely that females will have two altered copies of this gene, it is very rare for females to have hemophilia. A characteristic of X-linked inheritance is that fathers cannot pass X-linked traits to their sons. So, the correct answer is 'Mutation of X-chromosome'.

questions.text: A child with AB blood group who was born to a mother of the genotype IAIA cannot have the father of the genotype:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If the father is IA IA, he can only contribute IA alleles.The child would then receive IA from both parents → genotype IA IA, which corresponds to blood group A.However, the child’s blood group is AB, which requires a B allele.Hence, a father with IA IA genotype cannot produce an AB child.
If the father is IB IB, he can only pass on IB alleles.The child would get IA from the mother (IA IA) and IB from the father (IB IB).This produces IA IB, which gives the AB blood group

If the father is IB IO, he can contribute either IB or IO.If he passes IB, and the mother passes IA, the child’s genotype becomes IA IB, which corresponds to AB blood group.Hence, this combination is also possible.
Both IB IB and IB IO fathers can contribute a B allele, leading to IA IB in the child (AB blood group).Therefore, both these genotypes are possible for the father.

questions.explanation: A is the correct option because the genotype of the mother is IA IA. A child with blood group AB will have a genotype IA IB. This genotype of the child is possible in 2 cases either if the genotype of the father is IB IB or IB IO. However a genotype IA IA of the father will not result in the AB blood group of the child. Thus the correct answer is option A.

questions.text: An Rh-negative woman is married to an Rh-positive man whose father was also Rh-negative. What are the chances that their child will have Rh negative blood group?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If the man were homozygous for Rh-positive, there would be no chance for an Rh-negative child. However, since he is heterozygous, this percentage is incorrect.

Since the man is heterozygous for Rh-positive (carrying one Rh-positive and one Rh-negative allele), and the woman is homozygous Rh-negative, each child has a 50% chance of inheriting the Rh-negative allele from both parents.
This percentage would imply that both parents contribute a higher chance of an Rh-negative allele, which doesn't match the given genetic make-up.
This would only be possible if both parents were homozygous Rh-negative, which is not the case here.

questions.explanation: Given that the man’s father was Rh-negative and the man is Rh-positive, the man must be heterozygous for Rh-positive. A negative woman should be homozygous as a recessive allele only expresses itself in homozygous conditions. When a heterozygous dominant individual is crossed with a homozygous recessive individual, the offspring show the dominant and recessive phenotypes in a ratio of 1:1. Hence, there is a fifty percent chance of their child being “affected” with Rh negative phenotype.

questions.text: Locus stands for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Position of gene on homologous chromosomes
options.explanation: This option correctly describes a locus as the position on a chromosome where a gene is located. Homologous chromosomes have the same genes at the same locus, although they may carry different alleles.
This option is too broad. While a locus is a specific position on a chromosome, 'regions of chromosomes' refers to larger areas that can include multiple loci.

options.text: Position of an allele within a DNA molecule.
options.explanation: This option is partially correct. A locus is indeed the position of alleles (or genes) within a DNA molecule or on homologous chromosomes, so it complements Option A.

options.text: Close regions of the same chromosomes
options.explanation: This option inaccurately describes a locus. Close regions of the same chromosome pertain more to linkage rather than the specific position of a gene.

Locus is the point on a chromosome where the gene is situated. Homologous chromosomes have the same genes at the same locus but can have different alleles. Q. The position of a gene on a chromosome is called a locus. Both options A and C are correct.

questions.explanation: The correct answers are Options A and C. A locus refers to the specific position of a gene on a chromosome, and homologous chromosomes have the same genes at the same loci, but may carry different alleles, which is covered by both options. Option B is incorrect as it does not specify the precise nature of a locus. Option D is incorrect as it confuses the concept of a locus with genomic proximity unrelated to specific gene positions.

questions.text: Phenotype is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The genetic component i.e. the genes in an individual for a particular trait
options.explanation: This statement is more aligned with the concept of genotype rather than phenotype. The genotype represents the genetic makeup of an individual, including the specific alleles at various gene loci.

This is not an accurate definition of phenotype. Phenotype refers to the observable characteristics of an organism, influenced by both genetic factors (alleles) and environmental influences. It is not specifically the "partner" of a gene pair but rather the expression of those genes.

options.text: The form of appearance of a trait
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Phenotype is the observable form of a trait in an organism, encompassing physical and biochemical characteristics. It results from the interaction between an organism's genotype and the environment.

options.text: The position of a gene on the chromosome
options.explanation: This statement describes the concept of gene location or gene mapping, not phenotype. Phenotype is concerned with the expression of genes, not their specific positions on chromosomes.

questions.explanation: A phenotype is an individual's observable traits, such as height, eye color, and blood type.

questions.text: _ alleles both have an effect on the phenotype of a heterozygous organism:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Complete dominance is a form of dominance in the heterozygous condition wherein the allele that is regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. For instance, for an individual carrying two alleles that are both dominant (e.g. AA), the trait that they represent will be expressed.

Complete dominance is a form of dominance in the heterozygous condition wherein the allele that is regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. For instance, for an individual carrying two alleles that are both dominant (e.g. AA), the trait that they represent will be expressed.
Codominance, as it relates to genetics, refers to a type of inheritance in which two versions (alleles) of the same gene are expressed separately to yield different traits in an individual.

Multiple alleles can be defined as a series of forms of a gene situated at the same locus of homologous chromosomes. According to Mendel, each gene had two alternate forms or allele morphs are being dominant and the other being recessive.

questions.explanation: In a Co-dominant scenario, the two different alleles of the gene contributing to a particular characteristic would express themselves in a collaborative manner, producing a phenotype that is a combination of the two different phenotypes coded by the respective alleles.

questions.text: In the ABO system of blood groups, if both antigens are present but no antibody, the blood group of the individual would be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood group A has only A antigen and anti-B antibodies, not both antigens.
Blood group B has only B antigen and anti-A antibodies, not both antigens.
Blood group AB has both A and B antigens with no antibodies, matching the criteria.
Blood group O lacks both A and B antigens and has both anti-A and anti-B antibodies.

questions.explanation: Blood group AB is unique in the ABO blood group system as it contains both A and B antigens on the surface of red blood cells, but it does not have any antibodies against these antigens in the plasma. This means that an individual with blood group AB can receive blood from any other ABO blood group, making them universal recipients. On the other hand, blood group A has only the A antigen and anti-B antibodies, blood group B has only the B antigen and anti-A antibodies, and blood group O lacks both antigens but has both anti-A and anti-B antibodies. Thus, the correct answer is AB.

questions.text: Person with blood group AB is considered as universal recipient because he has:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both A and B antigens on RBC but no antibodies in the plasma
options.explanation: A person with blood group AB has both A and B antigens on the surface of their red blood cells (RBCs). Antigens are substances that can trigger an immune response in the body. In this case, since the person has both A and B antigens, their immune system does not produce antibodies against these antigens. As a result, when receiving blood transfusions, the person's plasma will not contain antibodies that would attack and destroy the transfused blood cells. Therefore, a person with blood group AB is considered a universal recipient because they can receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, AB, and O without an immune response.

options.text: Both A and B antibodies in the plasma
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If a person has both A and B antibodies in their plasma, it means they have blood group O. Blood group O individuals have neither A nor B antigens on their RBCs, but they have antibodies against both A and B antigens in their plasma. Therefore, they cannot receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, or AB.

options.text: No antigen on RBC and no antibody in the plasma
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If a person has no antigens on their RBCs and no antibodies in their plasma, it means they have blood group O. Blood group O individuals have no antigens on their RBCs, but they have antibodies against both A and B antigens in their plasma. Therefore, they cannot receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, or AB.

options.text: Both A and B antigens in the plasma but no antibodies
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Antigens are present on the surface of RBCs, not in the plasma. Having A and B antigens in the plasma is not a recognized blood group classification. The presence of antigens on RBCs and antibodies in the plasma determines a person's blood group compatibility.

questions.explanation: A person with blood group AB has both A and B antigens on the surface of their red blood cells (RBCs). Antigens are substances that can trigger an immune response in the body. In this case, since the person has both A and B antigens, their immune system does not produce antibodies against these antigens. As a result, when receiving blood transfusions, the person's plasma will not contain antibodies that would attack and destroy the transfused blood cells. Therefore, a person with blood group AB is considered a universal recipient because they can receive blood from individuals with blood groups A, B, AB, and O without an immune response.

questions.text: The traits that show autosomal inheritance are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Equal probabilities exist in both male and female individuals.
options.explanation: Autosomal genes are found on chromosomes 1–22, not on X or Y chromosomes.Since both males (XY) and females (XX) have two copies of each autosome, the chance of inheriting an autosomal trait is the same for both sexes.Autosomal inheritance can be either dominant or recessive:Autosomal dominant: Only one copy of the gene is needed for the trait to appear (e.g., Huntington’s disease, achondroplasia).Autosomal recessive: Two copies are needed (one from each parent) for the trait to appear (e.g., cystic fibrosis, sickle cell anemia).

options.text: Different probabilities in male and female individuals
options.explanation: If a trait shows different probabilities in males and females, it means it is sex-linked (either X-linked or Y-linked), not autosomal.For example, color blindness and hemophilia show higher occurrence in males because they are X-linked, not autosomal.

options.text: More probabilities in male than female individuals
options.explanation: If males are more affected, it suggests the gene is on the X chromosome (since males have only one X and one Y), not on autosomes.Thus, this pattern is typical of X-linked recessive traits, not autosomal ones.

options.text: More probabilities in female than male individuals
options.explanation: No autosomal trait shows greater occurrence in females unless influenced by hormonal or environmental factors.Autosomal inheritance depends only on the non-sex chromosomes, which are the same in both sexes.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct as Autosomal inheritance refers to the inheritance of a gene located on one of the 22 pairs of non-sex chromosomes in the human genome. When a gene is located on an autosome, it can be inherited in one of two ways: dominant or recessive. In dominant inheritance, only one copy of the mutated gene is required to express the trait or disorder. This means that if a parent carries the mutated gene, there is a 50% chance with each pregnancy that the child will inherit the mutation and therefore express the trait or disorder. Examples of autosomal dominant disorders include Huntington's disease and achondroplasia. In recessive inheritance, two copies of the mutated gene are required to express the trait or disorder. This means that if both parents are carriers of the mutated gene, there is a 25% chance with each pregnancy that their child will inherit two copies of the mutated gene and therefore develop the condition. Examples of autosomal recessive disorders include cystic fibrosis and sickle cell anemia.

questions.text: When hybrids are crossed, the genotype ratio of the offspring is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A 1:1 ratio occurs when a heterozygous individual is crossed with a homozygous recessive (Tt × tt), not when two hybrids are crossed.Hence, this ratio does not apply to a hybrid × hybrid cross.

A 3:1 ratio represents the phenotypic ratio, not the genotypic ratio.When hybrids are crossed (Tt × Tt):3 show the dominant trait (TT + Tt + Tt)1 shows the recessive trait (tt)So, phenotypically 3:1 — but the question asks for the genotype ratio.

This is the correct answer.When two hybrids (Tt × Tt) are crossed, the offspring genotypes occur in this proportion:1 TT (homozygous dominant)2 Tt (heterozygous)1 tt (homozygous recessive)This ratio follows Mendel’s Law of Segregation, which states that alleles separate during gamete formation and recombine at random during fertilization.
No such genotypic or phenotypic ratio exists in any basic Mendelian monohybrid cross.This is biologically incorrect.
This ratio does not appear in any Mendelian inheritance pattern.It is neither a genotype nor a phenotype ratio for a monohybrid cross.

questions.explanation: This is known as a monohybrid cross, involving the inheritance of a single trait (e.g., one gene with two alleles). The genotype ratio can be represented as follows:• 1/4 of the offspring are homozygous dominant (e.g., TT).• 1/2 of the offspring are heterozygous (e.g., Tt).• 1/4 of the offspring are homozygous recessive (e.g., tt).This ratio is based on Mendel's law of segregation, which describes how alleles segregate during gamete formation.

questions.text: Which of the following is a universal donor?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because blood group A has antigen A present on it so can only donate blood to Type A and AB.
Option B is incorrect because blood group B has antigen B present on it so can only donate blood to Type B and AB.
Option C is incorrect because blood group AB has antigen AB present on it so can only donate blood to Type AB.

Option D is the correct option because people with type O blood are called universal donors because their donated red blood cells have no A, B or Rh antigens and can therefore be safely given to people of any blood group.

questions.explanation: Option D is the correct option because people with type O blood are called universal donors because their donated red blood cells have no A, B or Rh antigens and can therefore be safely given to people of any blood group. Option A is incorrect because blood group A has antigen A present on it so can only donate blood to Type A and AB. Option B is incorrect because blood group B has antigen B present on it so can only donate blood to Type B and AB. Option C is incorrect because blood group AB has antigen AB present on it so can only donate blood to Type AB.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most suitable term for the genotype AaBb?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In homozygous, all genotypes are the same and in recessive all are written in small letters.
In homozygous dominant, all genotypes are the same and written in capital letters with the dominant form
This is correct as genotypes are heterozygous.
This is incorrect as genotypes are heterozygous.

questions.explanation: It will be heterozygous since the alleles are of 2 types.

questions.text: A man receives his X chromosome from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: His mother only: This option is correct. A man receives his X chromosome from his mother. This is because males inherit one X chromosome from their mother, which determines their sex.

options.text: Part from his father and part from his mother
options.explanation: Part from his father and part from his mother: This option is not accurate. In mammals, including humans, the sex chromosomes are not made up of parts from both parents. The Y chromosome determines male sex, while the X chromosome determines female sex. Males inherit one X chromosome from their mother and one Y chromosome from their father.
His father only: This option is not accurate. Males inherit their Y chromosome from their father, which determines their sex, but they inherit their X chromosome from their mother.

options.text: Either his mother or his father, It depends upon luck
options.explanation: Either his mother or his father, It depends upon luck: This option is not accurate. The inheritance of sex chromosomes is not based on luck. The specific combination of sex chromosomes (XX for female and XY for male) follows specific genetic patterns.

questions.explanation: Biologically male people always inherit their X chromosome from their mother, and Y chromosome from their father.

questions.text: The alternative form of a given gene is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A trait is a specific characteristic of an individual.
A phenotype is the appearance of a trait. Each person has its specific phenotype.

The alternative form of a gene is called an allele. An individual inherits two alleles one from each parent. For any given genomic location where such variations occur., if two alleles are the same then the person is homozygous, and if different alleles are present then the person is heterozygous.
"Genotype" refers to the genetic makeup of an organism; in other words, it describes an organism's complete set of genes.

questions.explanation: The alternative form of a gene is called an allele. An individual inherits two alleles one from each parent. For any given genomic location where such variations occur., if two alleles are the same then the person is homozygous, and if different alleles are present then the person is heterozygous.

questions.text: A man who has type AB blood cannot father a child with type _ blood, because he would pass on either the _ or the B allele to all of his offspring.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) A, O – IncorrectThe statement says the man cannot father a child with type A or type O.But this is wrong because he can have a child with type A if the other parent contributes an "A" or "O" allele (e.g., if the mother is type A or O).However, he cannot have a child with type O, so the "O" part is correct, but "A" makes this option incorrect

The correct answer is:b) O, AExplanation:Blood type is determined by A, B, and O alleles.A person with AB blood type has both the A and B alleles (genotype AB).This means they can only pass on either the A allele or the B allele to their child.For a child to have type O blood, they must inherit two O alleles (OO genotype)—one from each parent.Since an AB parent does not have an O allele, they cannot pass it on, meaning they cannot have a child with type O blood.Thus, a man with type AB blood cannot father a child with type O blood, because he only passes on either A or B alleles.So, the correct answer is b) O, A. ✅

c) B, O – IncorrectThe statement suggests he cannot have a child with type B or O.But he can have a child with type B if the mother contributes a B or O allele.Again, he cannot have a child with type O, but including B in the option makes it incorrect.

d) B, A – IncorrectThis statement says he cannot have a child with type B or type A, but this is wrong because he can have children with both A and B blood types, depending on the other parent's alleles.Since both A and B are possible, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The father can pass on either the A or B allele to each of his offspring. Even if the mother has blood type O, the child cannot have blood type O because of the A or B allele received from the father. A and B alleles are dominant over O. For blood type O (the recessive phenotype) to occur, there should be two copies of the O allele, one from each parent. This is impossible in the given situation.

questions.text: The gene for ABO-blood group systems in humans is represented by symbol
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The symbol "X" is not used to represent the gene for the ABO blood group system in humans.
It is represented by I as it has three alleles such as IA IB and i which represents blood group A B and O
The symbol "Y" is used to represent the Y chromosome in humans and is not related to the ABO blood group system.
The symbol "O" is used to represent the O blood type in the ABO blood group system. It represents the absence of A and B antigens on the surface of red blood cells.

questions.explanation: The gene for ABO blood group systems is represented by I, with the three alleles being IA, IB, and i.

questions.text: Which trait in human in an example of multiple alleles?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Eye color is an example of polygenic inheritance, where multiple genes contribute to the trait, but not multiple alleles for a single gene.
Incorrect. Skin color is also influenced by multiple genes and polygenic inheritance, not multiple alleles.

The human ABO blood type is a good example of multiple alleles. Humans can have red blood cells that are of type A (IA), type B (IB), or type O (i). These three different alleles can be combined in different ways following Mendel's Laws of Inheritance.
Incorrect. The Rh blood group system involves a single gene with two main alleles (Rh+ and Rh-), not multiple alleles.

questions.explanation: The human ABO blood type is a good example of multiple alleles. Humans can have red blood cells that are of type A (IA), type B (IB), or type O (i). These three different alleles can be combined in different ways following Mendel's Laws of Inheritance.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of an X-linked recessive trait in humans?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. This condition is actually X-linked dominant, not recessive. It leads to low phosphate levels and affects bone development.

The gene responsible for color blindness is located on the X chromosome. In other words, red-green color blindness is an X-linked recessive condition. If a female inherits one normal color vision gene and one mutated gene, she won't be red-green color blind, because it's a recessive trait.
Incorrect. Baldness (androgenetic alopecia) is a complex trait influenced by multiple genes and hormones, not solely X-linked.
Incorrect. Beard growth is primarily influenced by hormones like testosterone and is not determined by a single X-linked gene.

questions.explanation: The gene responsible for color blindness is located on the X chromosome. In other words, red-green color blindness is an X-linked recessive condition. If a female inherits one normal color vision gene and one mutated gene, she won't be red-green color blind, because it's a recessive trait.

questions.text: What type of statement is the following: 'What is your name?'
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence is a sentence that makes a statement, e.g, Strong wind is blowing today.
The imperative sentence is a type of sentence that forms a command or request, such as "Do not forget to clean your room."
An interrogative clause is a clause whose form is typically associated with question-like meanings, e.g, How are you?
An exclamatory sentence, also known as an exclamation sentence, is a statement that expresses strong emotion, e.g, Alas! His grandfather died.

questions.explanation: Since the given sentence is associated with a question-like meaning, it is an interrogative sentence, and hence, option C is accurate.

questions.text: Choose the sentence with correct punctuation:

options.text: However, it didn't take long for others to jump on board.

options.text: However it didn't take long for others to jump on board.

options.text: However, it didn't take long for others to jump on board?

options.text: However. It didn't take long for others to jump on board.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: "However, it didn't take long for others to jump on board." A comma is necessary after the introductory word "However" to create a pause and connect it properly to the main clause.

Is living is showing the present continuous tense. A present continuous tense denotes actions/events that are already in progress while we speak about them.

questions.text: Complete the series A2.5, B5, C7.5, _ ?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: D9 is incorrect because the series follows an alternating pattern of increasing by 2.5 and then by 2, so the next term should be 7.5 + 2.5 = 10.
2.5 is added to each number. 2.5+2.5=5 and 5+2.5=7.5 hence 7.5+2.5=10 and D is 10. So, option B is accurate.
D9.5 is incorrect because it doesn't fit the pattern, which involves adding 2.5 to the previous term.
D45 is incorrect because it is not part of the given pattern, and it doesn't follow the series progression of adding 2.5 to the previous term.

questions.explanation: 2.5 is added to each number. 2.5+2.5=5 and 5+2.5=7.5 hence 7.5+2.5=10 and D is 10. So, option B is accurate.

questions.text: Statement:I. Large number of Primary Schools in rural areas are run by only one teacher.II. There has been a huge dropout from primary schools in rural areas.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Statement I is the cause then 2 is its effect
options.explanation: One teacher running a lot of schools leads to mismanagement and it lowers the educational standard which causes students to drop out from school in rural areas. So, option A is the most accurate answer.

options.text: Statement 2 is the cause then I is its effect
options.explanation: Option B (Statement 2 is the cause, then I is its effect) is incorrect because the dropout from primary schools in rural areas can have various causes, and there is no indication that it directly causes the large number of schools run by one teacher.

Option C (Both statements are independent causes) is incorrect because the two statements do not appear to have any direct connection or correlation. They describe different situations without any clear causal relationship.

options.text: Both of the statements are effect of independent causes
options.explanation: Option D (Both of the statements are effects of independent causes) is incorrect because there is no evidence provided to suggest that both statements are effects of distinct, unrelated causes. Again, they seem to describe different aspects of primary education in rural areas without any evident causal link.

questions.explanation: One teacher running a lot of schools leads to mismanagement and it lowers the educational standard which causes students to drop out from school in rural areas. So, option A is the most accurate answer.

questions.text: Directions:In each of the following questions a statement is given, followed by two conclusions.Statement: "Please do not wait for me, I may be late, start taking lunch as soon as the guests arrive." - A message from a Director of a Company to his office managers.Assumptions:I. Keeping guests waiting is not desirable.II. Lunch may not be ready in time.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: The director instructs his managers not to keep the guests waiting because of him and to proceed with lunch soon after their arrival. This implies that lunch would be ready in time. So, only I is implicit. Hence, option A is accurate.

Option B is incorrect because assumption II is not implicit in the given statement. There is no indication in the statement that lunch may not be ready in time. The director only mentions being late, not the unavailability of lunch.

options.text: Either I or II is implicit
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because although assumption I is implicit, assumption II is not implicit, as explained above.
Option D is incorrect because it includes option B, which is itself incorrect. Therefore, option D cannot be the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The director instructs his managers not to keep the guests waiting because of him and to proceed with lunch soon after their arrival. This implies that lunch would be ready in time. So, only I is implicit. Hence, option A is accurate.
